q: Match the LIST-I with LIST-II Match the LIST-I with LIST-II List-I List-II A. Spring constant I. M L 2 T - 2 K - 1 M L 2 T - 2 K - 1 ML^(2)T^(-2)K^(-1) B. Thermal conductivity II. ML 0 T - 2 ML 0 T - 2 ML^(0)T^(-2) C. Boltzmann constant III. M L 2 T - 3 A - 2 M L 2 T - 3 A - 2 ML^(2)T^(-3)A^(-2) D. Inductive reactance IV. M L T - 3 K - 1 M L T - 3 K - 1 MLT^(-3)K^(-1) Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation: A. Spring constant (k) According to Hooke's Law, the restoring force F of a spring is directly proportional to its displacement x : F = - kx Taking the magnitude to find dimensions : k = F x ⇒ [ k ] = [ F x ] = [ MLT - 2 ] [ M 0 LT 0 ] [ k ] = [ MLT - 2 ] [ L ] = [ ML 1 - 1 T - 2 ] = [ ML 0 T - 2 ] A → II. B. Thermal conductivity (K) From Law of heat conduction, the rate of heat flow ( H = d Q dt ) is given by: H = K A Δ T L Where A is area, Δ T is temperature difference, and L is length. K = H ⋅ L A ⋅ Δ T The dimensions of rate of heat flow, Energy Time Force × distance Time [ H ] = [ Energy ] [ Time ] = [ Force × distance ] [ Time ] = [ MLT - 2 × L ] [ T ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 ] So the dimensions of thermal conductivity is, [ K ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 ] ⋅ [ L ] [ L 2 ] ⋅ [ K ] = [ ML 3 T - 3 ] [ L 2 K ] ⇒ [ K ] = [ ML 3 - 2 T - 3 K - 1 ] = [ MLT - 3 K - 1 ] B → IV. C. Boltzmann constant ( k B ) The average kinetic energy (E) of a gas molecule is related to its absolute temperature (T) by : E = 3 2 k B T The dimensionless constant is 3 2 . [ k B ] = [ E T ] We know Energy has the same dimensions as Work ( F × d ): [ E ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] So, the dimensions of Boltzmann constant is, [ k B ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] [ K ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 K - 1 ] C → I . D. Inductive reactance ( X L ) Inductive reactance is the opposition to alternating current by an inductor. It acts exactly like resistance, so it must have the same dimensional formula as electrical resistance (R). From Ohm's Law: V = IR , so R = V I . Potential is Work done per unit charge (W/q): Work Charge [ V ] = [ Work ] [ Charge ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] [ A ⋅ T ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 A - 1 ] So, the dimensions of inductive reactance, i.e., resistance is, [ X L ] = [ R ] = [ V ] [ I ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 A - 1 ] [ A ] [ X L ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 A - 2 ] D → III . Therefore, the correct match is, A → II, B → IV, C → I , D → III. Hence, the correct option is (D).

q: Consider the equation H = x p ϵ q E r t s Where H = magnetic field; E = electric field, ϵ = permittivity, x = distance, t = time The values of p , q , r and s respectively are :
explanation: To find the values of the exponents p , q , r , and s , we will use dimensional analysis. Both sides of the equation must have the same fundamental dimensions. The dimension of distance ( x ) is the dimension of length [ x ] = L The dimension is time [ t ] = T The dimension of electric field ( E ) can be derived from the formula for force on a charge, F = qE . Therefore, E = F / q . The dimensions of force is [ F ] = [ MLT - 2 ] Charge ( q ) = Current × Time So, the dimensions of charge is, [ q ] = [ AT ] So, the dimensions of electric field is, [ E ] = [ MLT - 2 ] [ AT ] = [ MLT - 3 A - 1 ] Using relationship between electric field (E) and and magnetic field (H) in electromagnetic waves. In a vacuum, the ratio of the magnitudes of the electric field to the magnetic field (B) is the speed of light (c), E / B = c Using the formula of magnetic force on a current-carrying conductor: F = BIL Where, F = force B = magnetic field (flux density), I = current and L = length B = F IL Substituting the dimensions, [ B ] = [ MLT - 2 ] [ LA ] ⇒ [ B ] = [ M T - 2 I - 1 ] The relation between B and H is B = μ H ⇒ B μ = H . Where μ = permeability. Using the formula of magnetic field, B = μ i 2 π r ⇒ [ B μ ] = [ i r ] , here r is distance from straight conductor carrying current i. [ B μ ] = [ H ] = [ A ] [ L ] = [ L - 1 A ] Putting the dimensions of electric field and speed of light, the dimensions of magnetic field is, [ H ] = [ MLT - 3 A - 1 ] [ LT - 1 ] = [ MT - 2 A - 1 ] We can derive the dimensions of permittivity ( ϵ ) from Coulomb's Law, F = 1 4 π ϵ q 1 q 2 r 2 . [ ϵ ] = [ q 2 Fr 2 ] [ ϵ ] = [ AT ] 2 [ MLT - 2 ] [ L 2 ] = [ M - 1 L - 3 T 4 A 2 ] Substituting the dimensions into the given equation, [ H ] = [ x ] p [ ϵ ] q [ E ] r [ t ] s ⇒ [ L - 1 A ] = [ L ] p [ M - 1 L - 3 T 4 A 2 ] q [ M L T - 3 A - 1 ] r [ T ] s [ L - 1 A ] = [ M - q + r L p - 3 q + r T 4 q - 3 r - s A 2 q - r ] On comparing the powers of M , L , T , and A on both sides, For M , - q + r = 0 … (i) For A , 2 q - r = 1 . Since r = q , then 2 q - q = 1 , so q = 1 and r = 1 . For L , p - 3 q + r = - 1 … (ii). p - 3 ( 1 ) + 1 = - 1 p - 2 = - 1 ⇒ p = 1 For T, 4 q - 3 r - s = 0 … (iii) 4 ( 1 ) - 3 ( 1 ) - s = 0 1 - s = 0 ⇒ s = 1 Therefore, the values are p = 1 , q = 1 , r = 1 , s = 1 . Hence, the correct option is (A).

q: Consider a modified Bernoulli equation. constant ( P + A B t 2 ) + ρ g ( h + B t ) + 1 2 ρ V 2 = constant If t has the dimension of time then the dimensions of A and B are _ _ _ _ , _ _ _ _ respectively.
options: A. [ ML 0 T - 1 ] and [ M 0 LT ]
B. [ ML 0 T - 2 ] and [ M 0 LT - 1 ]
C. [ ML 0 T - 2 ] and [ M 0 LT - 2 ]
D. [ ML 0 T - 1 ] and [ M 0 LT - 1 ]
explanation: In the term ( h + B t ) , B t is added to h , where h is the height or length which has the dimension: [ h ] = [ L ] . Therefore, using principle of homogeneity the product Bt must also have the dimension of length: [ L ] . Given that t is time whose dimension is [ T ] : [ B ] [ t ] = [ L ] ⇒ [ B ] [ T ] = [ L ] ⇒ [ B ] = [ L ] [ T ] = [ LT - 1 ] Therefore, the dimensions of B is [ M 0 LT - 1 ] . In the term ( P + A Bt 2 ) , A Bt 2 is added to P (Pressure). The dimension of pressure is Force Area P = Force Area : [ P ] = [ MLT - 2 ] [ L 2 ] = [ ML - 1 T - 2 ] According to the principle of homogeneity, the dimension of A Bt 2 must equal the dimension of P : [ A Bt 2 ] = [ ML - 1 T - 2 ] ⇒ [ A ] [ LT - 1 ] [ T 2 ] = [ ML - 1 T - 2 ] ⇒ [ A ] = [ ML - 1 T - 2 ] × [ LT ] ⇒ [ A ] = [ M ] [ L - 1 + 1 ] [ T - 2 + 1 ] ⇒ [ A ] = [ ML 0 T - 1 ] Therefore, dimensions of A is [ ML 0 T - 1 ] and the dimensions of B is [ M 0 LT - 1 ] . Hence, the correct option is (D).

q: If ϵ , E and t represent the free space permittivity, electric field and time respectively, then the unit of ϵ E t will be :
explanation: The SI unit of permittivity of free space ( ϵ ) is derived from Coulomb's law ( F = q 1 q 2 4 π ϵ 0 r 2 ) . The SI unit is C 2 / N . m 2 . The electric field (E) is defined as force per unit charge, so its SI unit is Newtons per Coulomb (N/C). The standard SI unit is the second (s). The given expression is ϵ E t . So, the SI unit of given expression is, C 2 N ⋅ m 2 × ( N C ) = C s = s s ⋅ m 2 Electric current is defined as the rate of flow of electric charge over time. Therefore, 1 Ampere ( A ) = 1 Coulomb per second ( C s ) . Unit of ϵ E t = ( C s ) × 1 m 2 = A × 1 m 2 = A / m 2 Therefore, the SI unit of ϵ E t is A / m 2 . Hence, the correct option is (C).

q: In a screw gauge when the circular scale is given five complete rotations it moves linearly by 2.5 mm . If the circular scale has 100 divisions, the least count of screw gauge is _ _ _ _ mm.
explanation: The pitch of a screw gauge is defined as the linear distance moved by the screw on the main scale when the circular head is given one complete rotation. The screw moves linearly by 2.5 mm when given five complete rotations. Therefore, the distance moved in a single rotation is : Pitch Total linear distance moved Number of full rotations Pitch = Total linear distance moved Number of full rotations Pitch Pitch = 2.5 mm 5 = 0.5 mm The least count is the smallest distance that can be measured accurately using the instrument. It is the distance moved by the screw when the circular scale is rotated through only one of its divisions. So, the least count of screw gauge is the ratio of the pitch to the total number of divisions on the circular scale: It is given that the circular scale has 100 divisions, L. C . = 0.5 mm 100 = 0.005 mm = 5 × 10 - 3 mm Therefore, the least count of given screw gauge is 5 × 10 - 3 mm . Thus, the correct option is (D).

q: The dimensional formula of 1 2 ϵ 0 E 2 ( ϵ 0 = permittivity of vacuum and E = electric field) is M a L b T c . The value of 2 a - b + c = ________.
explanation: The expression given is 1 2 ε 0 E 2 , which represents the energy density ( U d ) of an electric field in a vacuum. Energy density is defined as the energy stored per unit volume. Energy Volume U d = Energy ( U ) Volume ( V ) Energy is equivalent to work done, which is Force × Displacement. The dimensions of force is [ F ] = [ m a ] = [ M L T - 2 ] So, the dimensions of energy is [ U ] = [ M L T - 2 ] × [ L ] = [ M L 2 T - 2 ] The dimensions of volume is [ V ] = [ L ] × [ L ] × [ L ] = [ L 3 ] Since energy density is U V , its dimensions are: [ U d ] = [ M L 2 T - 2 ] [ L 3 ] = [ M L - 1 T - 2 ] The dimensional formula of energy density due to electric field is is M a L b T c . By comparing this with derived dimensional formula [ M 1 L - 1 T - 2 ] , we get: a = 1 , b = - 1 , c = - 2 So, the value of 2 a - b + c = 2 ( 1 ) - ( - 1 ) + ( - 2 ) = 2 + 1 - 2 = 1 Therefore, the value of 2 a - b + c is 1. Thus, the correct options is (B).

q: In a screw gauge, the zero of the circular scale lies 3 divisions above the horizontal pitch line when their metallic studs are brought in contact. Using this instrument thickness of a sheet is measured. If pitch scale reading is 1 mm and the circular scale reading is 51 then the correct thickness of the sheet is _ _ _ _ mm. [Assume least count is 0.01 mm ]
explanation: The circular scale lies 3 divisions above the horizontal pitch line when the studs are in contact. When the zero mark is above the reference line, the screw has been turned past the zero point. This indicates a negative zero error. The error corresponds to 3 divisions. Hence, the zero error is, Least Count E 0 = - ( 3 × Least Count ) = - ( 3 × 0.01 mm ) = - 0.03 mm The observed reading is the sum of the Pitch Scale Reading (PSR) and the Circular Scale Reading (CSR) multiplied by the Least Count (LC). The given Pitch Scale Reading (PSR) is 1 mm and the Circular Scale Reading (CSR) is 51 So, the observed reading = PSR + ( CSR × LC ) Observed Reading = 1 mm + ( 51 × 0.01 mm ) Observed Reading = 1 mm + 0.51 mm Observed Reading = 1.51 mm To find the true thickness, we must subtract the zero error from the observed reading. Correct Reading = Observed Reading - Zero Error Correct Reading = 1.51 mm - ( - 0.03 mm ) Correct Reading = 1.51 mm + 0.03 mm Correct Reading = 1.54 mm Therefore, the correct thickness of the sheet is 1.54 mm. Hence, the correct option is (A).

q: In a vernier callipers, 50 vernier scale divisions are equal to 48 main scale divisions. If one main scale division = 0.05 mm , then the least count of the vernier callipers is _ _ _ _ mm.
explanation: Value of one Main Scale Division, 1 MSD = 0.05 mm Number of Vernier Scale Divisions m = 50 Number of Main Scale Divisions is n = 48 50 VSD = 48 MSD ⇒ 1 VSD = 48 50 MSD The Least Count of a Vernier instrument is the smallest measurement that can be taken accurately. Least Count (LC) of Vernier scale = 1 MSD -1 VSD Using the least count formula, LC = 1 MSD - 1 VSD ⇒ LC = 1 MSD - ( 48 50 ) MSD ⇒ LC = ( 1 - 48 50 ) MSD ⇒ LC = ( 50 - 48 50 ) MSD ⇒ LC = 2 50 MSD = 2 50 × 0.05 mm = 0.002 mm Therefore, the smallest measurement that this Vernier calliper can record is 0.002 mm . Hence, the correct option is (A).

q: The time period of a simple harmonic oscillator is T = 2 π k m . Measured value of mass ( m ) of the object is 10 g with an accuracy of 10 mg and time for 50 oscillations of the spring is found to be 60 s using a watch of 2 s resolution. Percentage error in determination of spring constant ( k ) is ________%.
explanation: The formula for the time period of simple harmonic oscillator is T = 2 π k m . ⇒ T 2 = ( 2 π ) 2 ( k m ) ⇒ k = T 2 ⋅ m 4 π 2 Using propagation of error, when quantities are multiplied or divided, their relative errors (percentage errors) are added. The constants have no error. Δ k k × 100 = ( Δ m m + 2 ⋅ Δ T T ) × 100 The measured value of mass is, m = 10 g Accuracy/Error in measurement of mass is, Δ m = 10 mg = 0.01 g So, percentage error in measurement of mass Δ m m × 100 % = 0.01 10 × 100 = 0.1 % The percentage error in the time period ( T ) is the same as the percentage error in the total time ( t ) measured for n oscillations. Total time measured is, t = 60 s Resolution/Error in measurement of time is, Δ t = 2 s So, percentage error in measurement of time is, Δ t t × 100 % = 2 60 × 100 % = 3.33 % So, using propagation formula: Percentage error in k = ( % error in m ) + 2 × ( % error in t ) = 0.1 % + 2 × 3.33 % = 6.76 % Therefore, the correct option is (D).

q: Four persons measure the length of a rod as 20.00 cm , 19.75 cm , 17.01 cm and 18.25 cm . The relative error in the measurement of average length of the rod is :
explanation: The average (mean) of the measured values is the true value for our error calculation. x ¯ = x 1 + x 2 + x 3 + x 4 4 Substituting the given values : x ¯ = 20.00 + 19.75 + 17.01 + 18.25 4 = 75.01 4 x ― = 18.7525 ≈ 18.75 cm The absolute error is the magnitude of the difference between each individual measurement and the mean value. | Δ x i | = | x i - x ― | ⇒ | Δ x 1 | = | 20.00 - 18.75 | = 1.25 ⇒ | Δ x 2 | = | 19.75 - 18.75 | = 1.00 ⇒ | Δ x 3 | = | 17.01 - 18.75 | = | - 1.74 | = 1.74 ⇒ | Δ x 4 | = | 18.25 - 18.75 | = | - 0.50 | = 0.50 Then the average of these absolute errors. Δ x ― = ∑ | Δ x i | 4 ⇒ Δ x ¯ = 1.25 + 1.00 + 1.74 + 0.50 4 = 4.494 4 ⇒ Δ x ― = 1.1225 ≈ 1.12 cm The relative error is defined as the ratio of the mean absolute error to the mean value. Relative Error = Δ x ― x ― Relative Error = 1.1225 18.7525 Relative Error ≈ 0.0598 Rounding to two decimal places, Relative Error ≈ 0.06 Therefore, the relative error in the measurement is 0.06 . Hence, the correct option is (C).

q: Match List - I with List - II. Match List - I with List - II. List - I List - I List - II List - II A. Boltzmann constant I. [ M - 1 L 3 T - 2 ] B. Stefan's constant II. [ M L 2 T - 1 ] C. Planck's constant III. [ ML 2 T - 2 K - 1 ] D. Gravitational constant IV. [ M L 0 T - 3 K - 4 ] Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation: For the dimensional equation of Boltzmann Constant ( k B ) The average kinetic energy (E) of a gas molecule is related to temperature (T) by the equation : E = 3 2 k B T To find the dimensions of k B : [ k B ] = [ E ] [ T ] Using the formula Work = Force × Distance, dimensions of energy is [ E ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] The dimensions of temperature is [ T ] = [ K ] So, [ k B ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] [ K ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 K - 1 ] Thus, (A) Boltzmann constant → (III) [ ML 2 T - 2 K - 1 ] For the dimensions of Stefan's Constant ( σ ) According to Stefan-Boltzmann law, the energy radiated per unit area per unit time ( P ) is: P = σ T 4 Where Energy Area× Time P = Energy Area× Time Dimensions of [ P ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] [ L 2 ] [ T ] = [ MT - 3 ] Dimensions of T 4 is [ K 4 ] So, the dimensions of Stefan's constant is [ σ ] = [ P ] [ T 4 ] = [ MT - 3 ] [ K 4 ] = [ MT - 3 K - 4 ] = [ ML 0 T - 3 K - 4 ] Thus, (B) Stefan's constant → (IV)[ ML 0 T - 3 K - 4 ] For the dimensions of Planck's Constant (h): The energy (E) of a photon is given by E = h ν , where ν is frequency (Time - 1 ). So, the dimension of Planck's constant is [ h ] = [ E ] [ v ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] [ T - 1 ] ⇒ [ h ] = [ ML 2 T - 1 ] Thus, (C) Planck's constant → (II)[ ML 2 T - 1 ] For the dimensions of Gravitational Constant (G): From Newton's law of gravitation F = G m 1 m 2 r 2 ⇒ G = F r 2 m 1 m 2 The dimensions of Force is [ F ] = [ MLT - 2 ] The dimensions of Distance squared is [ r 2 ] = [ L 2 ] The dimensions of mass squared is [ m 1 m 2 ] = [ M 2 ] So, the dimensions of Gravitational constant is [ G ] = [ MLT - 2 ] [ L 2 ] [ M 2 ] = [ M - 1 L 3 T - 2 ] Thus (D) Gravitational constant → (I) [ M - 1 L 3 T - 2 ] A → III , B → IV , C → II , D → I Therefore, the correct option is (C).

q: A new unit ( α ) of length is chosen such that it is equal to the speed of light in vacuum. What is the distance between Venus and Earth in terms of α units if light takes 6 min. 40 s to cover this distance?
explanation: To find the distance between two points when an object moves at a constant speed, we use formula, Distance = Speed × Time α is a new unit of length such that 1 unit ( α ) = speed of light in vacuum (c). The speed of light c is the distance light travels per unit of time (usually meters per second). second 1 α = c × ( 1 second ) The time taken for light to travel from Venus to Earth is 6 minutes and 40 seconds. 1 minute = 60 seconds 6 minutes = 6 × 60 = 360 seconds Total time t = 360 s + 40 s = 400 seconds Using distance formula: Distance = Speed of light × Time Distance = c × 400 seconds Since 1 α is the distance light travels in 1 second ( c × 1 s ), we can substitute c with our new unit, Distance = 400 × ( c × 1 s ) Distance = 400 α Therefore, the distance between Venus and Earth is 400 α . Hence, the correct option is (B).

q: A spherical body of radius r and density σ falls freely through a viscous liquid having density ρ and viscosity η and attains a terminal velocity v 0 . Estimated maximum error in the quantity η is : (Ignore errors associated with σ , ρ and g , gravitational acceleration)
options: A. 2 [ Δ r r - Δ v 0 v 0 ]
B. 2 [ Δ r r + Δ v 0 v 0 ]
C. 2 Δ r r + Δ v 0 v 0
D. 2 Δ r r - Δ v 0 v 0
explanation: When a spherical body falls through a viscous liquid, the forces acting on it are, Weight ( W ) which is acting downwards. Up thrust ( F b ) is acting upwards (due to buoyancy). Viscous Drag ( F v ) is acting upwards (opposing the motion). $JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Evening Shift Physics - Units & Measurements Question 30 English Explanation$ The radius of the spherical body is r . The density of the body is σ So, the weight of the spherical body is, W = mg = ( σ V ) g = σ [ 4 3 π r 3 ] g The density of the liquid is ρ , and when body is completely inside the liquid, the up thrust is, F B = ρ Vg = 4 3 π r 3 ρ g The viscous drag acting on the body is F v = 6 π η rv Terminal velocity is reached when the net force on the body becomes zero (acceleration is zero). W = F B + F v ⇒ 4 3 π r 3 σ g = 4 3 π r 3 ρ g + 6 π η r v 0 ⇒ 6 π η r v 0 = 4 3 π r 3 g ( σ - ρ ) ⇒ η = 4 3 π r 3 g ( σ - ρ ) 6 π r v 0 ⇒ η = 2 r 2 g ( σ - ρ ) 9 v 0 ⇒ η = [ 2 g ( σ - ρ ) 9 ] r 2 v 0 As it is given to ignore errors associated with σ , ρ , and g . Therefore, [ 2 g ( σ - ρ ) 9 ] is a constant ( k ) . η = k ⋅ r 2 v 0 Taking natural logarithm on both sides: ln ⁡ ( η ) = ln ⁡ ( k ) + 2 ln ⁡ ( r ) - ln ⁡ ( v 0 ) On differentiating both sides: d η η = 0 + 2 d r r - d v 0 v 0 For maximum error, we sum the absolute values of the individual errors because errors always propagate in a way that maximizes the uncertainty: or Δ η η = 2 Δ r r + Δ v 0 v 0 or Δ η η = - 2 Δ r r - Δ v 0 v 0 Therefore, the correct option is (C).

q: Keeping the significant figures in view, the sum of the physical quantities 52.01 m, 153.2 m and 0.123 m is :
explanation: In addition, or subtraction, the final result should be reported to the same number of decimal places as the measurement with the least number of decimal places. This ensures the result is no more precise than the least precise measurement. The given measured values are, 52.01 m : 2 decimal places. 153.2 m : 1 decimal place. 0.123 m : 3 decimal places. The measurement with the least number of decimal places is 153.2 m ( 1 decimal place). Therefore, our final answer must be rounded to one decimal place. 52.01 + 153.2 + 0.123 = 205.333 m Now, we round 205.333 to one decimal place : The digit at the first decimal place is 3 → 205.33 The digit following it is 3 (which is less than 5 ) → 205.3 Therefore, the rounded result is 205.3 m . Hence, the correct option is (A)

q: The density ρ of a uniform cylinder is determined by measuring its mass m , length l and diameter d . The measured values of m , l and d are 97.42 ± 0.02 g , 8.35 ± 0.05 mm and 20.20 ± 0.02 mm , respectively. Calculated percentage fractional error in ρ is _ _ _ _ .
explanation: The density ( ρ ) of a body is defined as the ratio of its mass (m) to its volume (V). ρ = m V For a uniform cylinder of length l and diameter D , the volume is, V = π r 2 l = π ( D 2 ) 2 l = π D 2 l 4 Substituting the volume into the density formula: ρ = m V = 4 m π D 2 l To find the fractional error, we take the natural logarithm ( ln ) on both sides: ln ⁡ ( ρ ) = ln ⁡ ( 4 ) + ln ⁡ ( m ) - ln ⁡ ( π ) - 2 ln ⁡ ( D ) - ln ⁡ ( l ) Differentiating both sides to find the relative change d ρ ρ = dm m - 2 dD D - dl l The maximum possible fractional error is the sum of the absolute values of the individual relative errors: Δ ρ ρ = Δ m m + 2 Δ D D + Δ l l For the percentage fractional error, Error in % Error in ρ = ( Δ m m + 2 Δ D D + Δ l l ) × 100 The measured value of mass is 97.42 ± 0.02 g ⇒ m = 97.42 g and the absolute error is Δ m = 0.02 g So, relative error in mass is, Δ m m = 0.02 97.42 ≈ 0.000205 The measured value of length is 8.35 ± 0.05 mm ⇒ l = 8.35 mm and the absolute error is Δ l = 0.05 mm So, relative error in length is, Δ l l = 0.05 8.35 ≈ 0.005988 The measured value of diameter is 20.20 ± 0.02 mm ⇒ D = 20.20 mm and the absolute error is Δ D = 0.02 mm So, relative error in diameter is, 2 Δ D D = 2 × 0.02 20.20 = 0.04 20.20 ≈ 0.001980 Putting the values in the expression for the percentage error in density, Δ ρ ρ × 100 % = ( 0.000205 + 0.005988 + 0.001980 ) × 100 % = 0.008173 × 100 % ⇒ Δ ρ ρ × 100 % = 0.8173 % ≈ 0.82 % Therefore, the calculated percentage fraction error in density is approximately 0.82 % . Hence, the correct option is (B).

q: In a Vernier calipers, when both jaws touch each other, zero of the Vernier scale is shifted to the right of zero of the main scale and th 7 th Vernier division coincides with a main scale reading. If the value of 1 main scale division is 1 mm and there are 10 Vernier scale divisions, then the Vernier caliper has
explanation: The Least Count is the smallest measurement that can be accurately taken using the instrument. For a Vernier calliper, the difference between the length of one Main Scale Division (MSD) and one Vernier Scale Division (VSD) is equal to the least count. LC = 1 MSD - 1 VSD It is given that 1 MSD = 1 mm . In a standard Vernier calliper arrangement, 10 divisions on the Vernier scale correspond to the length of 9 divisions on the Main scale. 10 VSD = 9 MSD ⇒ 1 VSD = 9 10 MSD = 0.9 mm So, the least count is; LC = 1 mm - 0.9 mm = 0.1 mm ⇒ LC = 0.1 mm = 0.01 cm Zero error occurs when the jaws of the calliper are completely closed but the zero mark of the main scale does not align with the zero mark of the Vernier scale. If the zero of the Vernier scale rests to the right of the main scale zero, the instrument will naturally measure a value larger than the true length and this is positive zero error. If the zero of the Vernier scale rests to the left of the main scale zero, it creates a negative zero error. It is given that the zero of the Vernier scale is shifted to the right. Therefore, the given Vernier scale is with a positive zero error. If Vernier division perfectly coincides with any main scale reading, then the zero error is, e = n × LC Where n is the coinciding division. The problem states that the th 7 th division coincides. e = 7 × 0.01 cm = 0.07 cm Since the shift is to the right, the final error is +0.07 cm . Thus, the correct option is (C).

q: Dimensions of universal gravitational constant ( G ) in terms of Planck's constant ( h ), distance ( L ), mass ( M ) and time ( T ) are _______.
explanation: To find the dimensions of the universal gravitational constant G in terms of Planck's constant h , length L , mass M , and time T , we must first determine the base dimensions of both G and h in the standard MLT system. According to Newton's law of universal gravitation, the force F between two masses m 1 and m 2 separated by a distance r is given by: F = Gm 1 m 2 r 2 ⇒ G = Fr 2 m 1 m 2 Substituting the dimensions [ M L T - 2 ] for force, [ L ] for distance ( r ) , and mass( m ) as [ M ] : [ G ] = [ MLT - 2 ] [ L 2 ] [ M ] [ M ] = [ M - 1 L 3 T - 2 ] Planck's constant relates the energy E of a photon to its frequency v via the equation: E = h ν Since frequency v has dimensions of inverse time [ T - 1 ], and energy E (work done) has dimensions [ ML 2 T - 2 ] , we have: [ h ] = [ E ] [ v ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] [ T - 1 ] = [ ML 2 T - 1 ] Let assume the relationship is of the form G = h a L b M c T d . Comparing the dimensions: [ M - 1 L 3 T - 2 ] = [ ML 2 T - 1 ] a [ L ] b [ M ] c [ T ] d [ M - 1 L 3 T - 2 ] = [ M a + c L 2 a + b T - a + d ] By equating the exponents of M , L , and T from both sides, we get a system of equations: For M : a + c = - 1 … (i) For L : 2 a + b = 3 … (ii) For T : - a + d = - 2 … (iii) Looking at the provided options, we see that h is with a power of 1 , a = 1 Let's a = 1 : From eq (i): 1 + c = - 1 ⇒ c = - 2 From eq (ii): 2 ( 1 ) + b = 3 ⇒ b = 1 From eq (iii): - 1 + d = - 2 ⇒ d = - 1 Substituting these values, we get G ∝ h 1 L 1 M - 2 T - 1 . This corresponds to the dimensional formula [ hT - 1 LM - 2 ] . Hence, the correct option is (B).

q: In an experiment, a set of reading are obtained as follows - 1.24 mm, 1.25 mm, 1.23 mm, 1.21 mm. The expected least count of the instrument used in recording these readings is _______ mm.
explanation: The least count of an instrument is the smallest measurement that can be taken accurately with that instrument. The least count is shown by the position of the last significant digit (the smallest decimal place) to which all readings are recorded. The obtained readings are : 1. Reading 1 : 1.24 mm 2. Reading 2 : 1.25 mm 3. Reading 3 : 1.23 mm 4. Reading 4 : 1.21 mm All the readings are recorded up to two decimal places. The difference between consecutive possible readings 1.25 mm - 1.24 mm = 0.01 mm 1.24 mm - 1.23 mm = 0.01 m If the least count were 0.1 mm (the smallest decimal place is 1 ), the readings would look like 1.2 mm or 1.3 mm . If the least count were 0.001 mm (the smallest decimal place is 3 ), the readings would look like 1.240 mm . If the least count were 0.05 mm , the readings would only end in .00 or .05 (e.g., 1.20 , 1.25 ). Since we have 1.24 and 1.23, this is not possible. Therefore, the least count of the instrument is 0.01 mm . Hence, the correct option is (A).

q: L , C and R represents physical quantities inductance, capacitance and resistance respectively. The dimensional formula ML 2 T - 4 A - 2 corresponds to _ _ _ _ .
explanation: Electric power ( P ) is defined as work done per unit time. In a resistor of resistance R if the current is I , then electric power is given as, P = I 2 R For the dimensions of force (F) = Mass × Acceleration = [ MLT - 2 ] For the dimensions of work ( W ) = Force × Distance = [ MLT - 2 ] × [ L ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] Hence, the dimensions of power are, [ P ] = [ W T ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] [ T ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 ] From the electric power formula, R = P I 2 ⇒ [ R ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 ] [ A 2 ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 A - 2 ] The energy (E) stored in a capacitor is E = 1 2 C V 2 . Electric Potential (V) is defined as work per unit charge, V = W Q , The dimensions of charge ( Q ) is [ Q ] = [ AT ] . So, the dimensions of potential is, [ V ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] [ AT ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 A - 1 ] Using the definition of capacitance C = Q V , the dimensions of capacitance is; [ C ] = [ A T ] [ M L 2 - 3 A - 1 ] = [ M - 1 L - 2 T 4 A 2 ] The energy (E) stored in an inductor is E = 1 2 LI 2 . The dimensions of energy are [ ML 2 T - 2 ] . So, the dimensions of inductance are, L = 2 E I 2 ⇒ [ L ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] A 2 = [ ML 2 T - 2 A - 2 ] Hence, the dimensions of inductance, capacitance, and resistance are, [ R ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 A - 2 ] , [ C ] = [ M - 1 L - 2 T 4 A 2 ] , [ L ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 A - 2 ] Let the dimensional formula [ ML 2 T - 4 A - 2 ] corresponds to R x L y C z Substituting these into the equation : [ M 1 L 2 T - 4 A - 2 ] = [ M L 2 T - 3 A - 2 ] x [ M L 2 T - 2 A - 2 ] y [ M - 1 L - 2 T 4 A 2 ] z [ M 1 L 2 T - 4 A - 2 ] = [ M x + y - z L 2 x + 2 y - 2 z T - 3 x - 2 y + 4 z A - 2 x - 2 y + 2 z ] Comparing the exponents for each fundamental unit: For mass, M : x + y - z = 1 … (i) For length, L : 2 x + 2 y - 2 z = 2 ⇒ x + y - z = 1 … (ii) For time, T : - 3 x - 2 y + 4 z = - 4 … (iii) For current, A : - 2 x - 2 y + 2 z = - 2 … (iv) From (i): x + y = 1 + z Substituting this into (ii) : - 3 x - 2 y + 4 z = - 4 - x - 2 ( x + y ) + 4 z = - 4 - x - 2 ( 1 + z ) + 4 z = - 4 - x - 2 - 2 z + 4 z = - 4 - x + 2 z = - 2 ⇒ x = 2 z + 2 … ( v ) From given options the possible exponents of resistance (R) are 1 (a and b), - 1 2 (c) or -1 (d). If x = 1 , z = ( x - 2 ) 2 = ( 1 - 2 ) 2 = - 1 2 ; then x + y = 1 + z ⇒ 1 + y = 1 - 1 2 ⇒ y = - 1 2 Then the expression will be R x L y C z = R 1 L - 1 / 2 C - 1 / 2 = R LC → (a) If x = - 1 2 , z = ( x - 2 ) 2 = ( - 1 2 - 2 ) 2 = - 5 4 ; then x + y = 1 + z ⇒ - 1 2 + y = 1 - 5 2 ⇒ y = - 1 Then the expression will be R x L y C z = R - 1 / 2 L - 1 C - 5 / 4 (doesn't match option (c)) If x = - 1 , z = ( x - 2 ) 2 = ( - 1 - 2 ) 2 = - 3 2 ; then x + y = 1 + z ⇒ - 1 + y = 1 - 3 2 ⇒ y = 1 2 Then the expression will be R x L y C z = R - 1 L 1 / 2 C 1 / 2 = LC R (doesn't match option (d)) Therefore, the dimensional formula [ M 1 L 2 T - 4 A - 2 ] corresponds to R LC .

q: The percentage error in the calculated volume of a sphere, if there is 2 % error in its diameter measurement, is _ _ _ _ .
explanation: The volume V of a sphere is given by the formula V = 4 3 π r 3 , where r is the radius of the sphere. Since the error is given in terms of diameter (D), we express the radius as r = D 2 . Substituting this into the volume formula: V = 4 3 π ( D 2 ) 3 ⇒ V = 4 3 π ( D 3 8 ) ⇒ V = 1 6 π D 3 To find the relative error, we take the natural logarithm ( ln ) on both sides of the equation : ln ⁡ ( V ) = ln ⁡ ( π 6 ) + ln ⁡ ( D 3 ) ⇒ ln ⁡ ( V ) = ln ⁡ ( π 6 ) + 3 ln ⁡ ( D ) Now, on differentiating both sides. dV V = 0 + 3 ( dD D ) ⇒ dV V = 3 dD D To express this as a percentage error, we multiply both sides by 100 : ( dV V × 100 ) = 3 × ( dD D × 100 ) It is given that the percentage error in the diameter is 2 % . d D D × 100 = 2 % So, percentage error in Volume = 3 × 2 % Percentage error in Volume = 6 % Therefore, the correct option is (C).

q: Match the LIST-I with LIST-II Match the LIST-I with LIST-II List - I List - II A. Planck's constant I. ML 2 T - 2 B. Stopping potential II. T - 1 C. Work function III. ML 2 T - 1 D. Threshold frequency IV. ML 2 T - 3 A - 1 Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation: For the dimensions of Planck's Constant (h) According to the photon energy equation E = h ν , where E is energy and ν is frequency. Planck's constant is given by h = E v The dimensions of energy (E) are [ E ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] . The dimensions of frequency ( v ) are [ v ] = [ T - 1 ] . Therefore, dimensions of h : [ h ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] [ T - 1 ] = [ ML 2 T - 1 ] Thus, A → III For the dimensions of stopping potential ( V 0 ) Stopping potential is defined as the potential difference required to stop the fastest moving electrons. The work done by the potential is equal to the kinetic energy, W = qV 0 , where q is charge. So, stopping potential is V 0 = w q . The dimensions of work ( W ) are [ W ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] . The dimensions of charge ( q = I × t ) are [ q ] = [ AT ] . Therefore, dimensions of stopping potential is: [ V 0 ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] [ AT ] = [ ML 2 T - 3 A - 1 ] Thus, B → IV. For the dimensions of work function ( ϕ ) The work function is the minimum energy required to eject an electron from a metal surface. Since it is a form of energy, its dimensions are the same as energy (E). So, the dimensions of work function is [ ϕ ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] Thus, C → I . For the dimensions of threshold frequency ( v 0 ) Threshold frequency is the minimum frequency of incident radiation required for photoelectric emission. Frequency is defined as the reciprocal of the time period ( v = 1 T ) . So, the dimensions of threshold frequency is [ v 0 ] = [ T - 1 ] Thus, C → II. Therefore, the correct match is A - III, B - IV, C - I, D - II. Hence, the correct option is (A).

q: In a screw gauge the zero of main scale reference line coincides with the fifth division of the circular scale when two studs are in contact. There are 100 divisions in circular scale and pitch of screw gauge is 0.1 mm. When diameter of a sphere is measured, the reading of main scale is 5 mm and 50 $^{th}$ division of circular scale coincides with the reference line of main scale. The diameter of sphere is ______ mm.
explanation: The least count is the smallest distance the screw moves when the circular scale is rotated by one division. It is defined as: Least Count Pitch Number of divisions on circular scale Least Count = Pitch Number of divisions on circular scale The pitch is linear distance moved in one complete rotation. The pitch of screw gauge is given as 0.1 mm and there are 100 divisions: So, the least count is LC = 0.1 mm 100 = 0.001 mm When the studs are in contact, the 5th division of the circular scale coincides with the reference line. Since the zero of the circular scale has passed the reference line, the error is positive. Zero Error = + ( Coinciding division × LC ) ⇒ Zero Error = + ( 5 × 0.001 mm ) = + 0.005 mm The total observed reading is the sum of the Main Scale Reading (MSR) and the Circular Scale Reading (CSR). MSR = 5 mm CSR = Coinciding circular division × LC = 50 × 0.001 mm = 0.05 mm So, observed reading = 5 mm + 0.05 mm = 5.05 mm The true diameter is obtained by subtracting the zero error from the observed reading: So, the true diameter = Observed Reading - Zero Error True Diameter = 5.05 mm - 0.005 mm = 5.045 mm Therefore, the correct option is (A).

q: In an experiment the values of two spring constants were measured as k 1 = ( 10 ± 0.2 ) N / m and k 2 = ( 20 ± 0.3 ) N / m . If these springs are connected in parallel, then the percentage error in equivalent spring constant is :
explanation: When two springs are connected in parallel, they both undergo the same displacement (x) when a force is applied. $JEE Main 2026 (Online) 21st January Morning Shift Physics - Units & Measurements Question 21 English Explanation$ Force in spring 1 is F 1 = k 1 x Force in spring 2 is F 2 = k 2 x The total force F is the sum of individual forces : F = F 1 + F 2 ⇒ k eq x = k 1 x + k 2 x ⇒ k eq = k 1 + k 2 Substituting the given values, k 1 = 10 N / m , k 2 = 20 N / m k eq = 10 + 20 = 30 N / m When two measured quantities are added, the absolute error in the result is the sum of the absolute errors of the individual quantities. Δ k eq = Δ k 1 + Δ k 2 Substituting the given values, Δ k 1 = 0.2 N / m , Δ k 2 = 0.3 N / m Δ k eq = 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.5 N / m So, the percentage error in measurement of equivalent spring constant is, Percentage Error = ( Δ k eq k eq ) × 100 % = ( 0.5 30 ) × 100 % ≈ 1.666 … % Rounding to two decimal places, we get the percentage error in measurement of spring constant as 1.67 % . Therefore, the equivalent spring constant with its error is ( 30 ± 0.5 ) N / m , which corresponds to a percentage error of 1.67 % . Hence, the correct option is (C).

q: The speed of a longitudinal wave in a metallic bar is 400 m/s. If the density and Young's modulus of the bar material are increased by 0.5% and 1%, respectively then the speed of the wave is changed approximately to ______ m/s.
explanation: The formula for the speed of a longitudinal wave ( v ) in a solid metallic bar is given as v = Y ρ Where, Y = Young's modulus and its ρ is its density Taking the natural log on both sides of v = ( Y ρ ) 1 / 2 : ln ⁡ ( v ) = 1 2 ln ⁡ ( Y ) - 1 2 ln ⁡ ( ρ ) Differentiating both sides gives the fractional change: Δ v v = 1 2 Δ Y Y - 1 2 Δ ρ ρ Initial speed = v = 400 m / s Increase in Young's modulus = Δ Y Y × 100 = + 1 % Increase in density Δ ρ ρ × 100 = + 0.5 % So, the percentage change in speed, change in % change in v = 1 2 ( 1 % ) - 1 2 ( 0.5 % ) = 0.5 % - 0.25 % = 0.25 % Since the result is positive, the speed increases by 0.25 % . So, new speed ( v ′ ) = Initial Speed + Change in Speed of v ′ = v + ( 0.25 % of v ) ⇒ v ′ = 400 + ( 0.25 100 × 400 ) ⇒ v ′ = 400 + 1 = 401 m / s Hence, the correct option is (C).

q: When both jaws of vernier callipers touch each other, zero mark of the vernier scale is right to zero mark of main scale, th 4 th mark on vernier scale coincides with certain mark on the main scale. While measuring the length of a cylinder, observer observes 15 divisions on main scale and th 5 th division of vernier scale coincides with a main scale division. Measured length of cylinder is _ _ _ _ mm. (Least count of Vernier calliper = 0.1 mm )
explanation: When jaws touch, the vernier zero is to the right of the main scale zero, which indicates a positive zero error. Coinciding vernier division (CVD) for error = 4 . Least Count ( LC ) = 0.1 mm . So, zero error = + ( CVD × LC ) = + ( 4 × 0.1 ) = + 0.4 mm Main scale reading ( MSR ) = 15 divisions. Assuming 1 division = 1 mm , then main scale reading is MSR = 15 mm Vernier Scale Reading ( VSR ) = ( Coinciding vernier division ) × LC . VSR = 5 × 0.1 = 0.5 mm Total Observed Reading = MSR + VSR = 15 + 0.5 = 15.5 mm True Length = Observed Reading - Zero Error True Length = 15.5 mm - ( + 0.4 mm ) True Length = 15.1 mm Hence, the correct option is (D).

q: The diameter of a wire measured by a screw gauge of least count 0.001 cm is 0.08 cm. The length measured by a scale of least count 0.1 cm is 150 cm. When a weight of 100 N is applied to the wire, the extension in length is 0.5 cm, measured by a micrometer of least count 0.001 cm. The error in the measured Young’s modulus is α × 10 9 N / m 2 . The value of α is ________. (Ignore the contribution of the load to Young’s modulus error calculation)
explanation: Young's modulus ( Y ) is defined as the ratio of longitudinal stress to longitudinal strain. Let F be the force, L the original length, A the cross-sectional area, and Δ L the extension. Stress Strain Y = Stress Strain = F / A Δ L / L = F L A Δ L For a wire of diameter D , the area A = π D 2 4 . Substituting this into the formula: Y = F L ( π D 2 4 ) Δ L = 4 F L π D 2 Δ L To find the absolute error in Y , we need to find the relative error. Since F and π are constants, the relative error in Y is: scale Δ Y Y = Δ L scale L + 2 Δ D D + Δ ( Δ L ) Δ L Where scale Δ L scale , Δ D , and Δ ( Δ L ) are the least counts of the respective measuring instruments. The applied tensile force is N F = 100 N , The length of wire is cm m L = 150 cm = 1.5 m , The diameter of wire is cm m D = 0.08 cm = 8 × 10 - 4 m , The extension in the length of wire is cm m Δ L = 0.5 cm = 5 × 10 - 3 m . Y = 4 × 100 × 1.5 π × ( 8 × 10 - 4 ) 2 × 5 × 10 - 3 Y = 600 π × 64 × 10 - 8 × 5 × 10 - 3 = 600 π × 320 × 10 - 11 = 6 × 10 11 3.2 π N/m Y ≈ 5.96 × 10 10 N/m 2 So, the error in measurement of Young’s modulus is ( Δ Y ) : Δ Y = Y [ 0.1 150 + 2 ( 0.001 0.08 ) + 0.001 0.5 ] Δ Y = Y [ 0.00066 + 0.025 + 0.002 ] = Y [ 0.02766 ] N/m Δ Y = ( 5.96 × 10 10 ) × 0.02766 ≈ 1.65 × 10 9 N/m 2 Therefore, the value of α is 1.65 . Thus, the correct option is (B).

q: The potential energy of a particle changes with distance x from a fixed origin as V = A x x + B , where A and B are constant with appropriate dimensions. The dimensions of A B are _ _ _ _
explanation: According to the principle of homogeneity, dimensions of each term on both sides of a equation must be the same. Also, only physical quantities of the same dimensions can be added or subtracted. The potential energy of the particle is given as V = A x x + B , where A and B are constant. In the denominator of the expression x + B , the constant B is added to distance x . Therefore, the dimensions of B must be the same as the dimensions of x . [ B ] = [ x ] = [ L 1 ] The potential energy V has the same dimensions as work or energy. [ V ] = [ F × x ] = [ M 1 L 1 T - 2 × L 1 ] = [ M 1 L 2 T - 2 ] On rearranging the formula of potential energy for A : A = V ( x + B ) x Substituting the dimensions: [ A ] = [ M 1 L 2 T - 2 ] [ L 1 ] [ L 1 / 2 ] ⇒ [ A ] = [ M 1 L 3 T - 2 ] [ L 1 / 2 ] ⇒ [ A ] = [ M 1 L 3 - 1 2 T - 2 ] = [ M 1 L 5 / 2 T - 2 ] To find the dimensions of the product AB : [ AB ] = [ A ] × [ B ] ⇒ [ AB ] = [ M 1 L 5 / 2 T - 2 ] × [ L 1 ] ⇒ [ AB ] = [ M 1 L 5 / 2 + 1 T - 2 ] ⇒ [ AB ] = [ M 1 L 7 / 2 T - 2 ] Therefore, the dimensions of A B is [ M 1 L 7 / 2 T - 2 ] . Thus, the correct option is (D).

q: In an experiment to determine the resistance of a given wire using Ohm's law, the voltmeter and ammeter readings are noted as 10 V and 5 A , respectively. The least counts of voltmeter and ammeter are 500 mV and 200 mA , respectively. The estimated error in the resistance measurement is _ _ _ _ Ω
explanation: According to Ohm's law, the resistance R is given by the ratio of potential difference V to the current I : R = V I The reading of voltmeter is V = 10 V and that of ammeter is I = 5 A Then resistance is R = 10 5 = 2 Ω When a quantity is determined by the division of two other quantities ( R = V I ) , the relative error in the result is the sum of the relative errors of the individual quantities. The relative error is, Δ R R = Δ V V + Δ I I Where Δ R is the absolute error in resistance, Δ V is the error in voltage, and Δ I is the error in current. The error in a measurement is taken as the least count of the instrument used. So, error in voltmeter is Δ V = 500 mV = 0.5 V And the error in ammeter is Δ I = 200 mA = 0.2 A Substituting values into the error equation Δ R 2 = 0.5 10 + 0.2 5 ⇒ Δ R 2 = 0.05 + 0.04 = 0.09 ⇒ Δ R = 0.09 × 2 = 0.18 Ω Therefore, the estimated error in the resistance measurement is 0.18 Ω . Hence, the correct option is (D).

q: Match List - I with List - II . List - I List - II (A) Young’s Modulus (I) M L $^{-1}$ T $^{-1}$ (B) Torque (II) M L $^{-1}$ T $^{-2}$ (C) Coefficient of Viscosity (III) M $^{-1}$ L $^{3}$ T $^{-2}$ (D) Gravitational Constant (IV) M L $^{2}$ T $^{-2}$ Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation: We know, Young's modulus, E = σ ε ⇒ E = F A Δ L L = F A ( L Δ L ) ⇒ [ E ] = [ F ] [ A ] = [ M 1 L 1 T - 2 ] [ L 2 ] (A) ⇒ [ E ] = [ M 1 L - 1 T - 2 ] (B) we know, Torque = r × F [ τ ] = [ r ] [ F ] = [ L ] [ M 1 L 1 T - 2 ] [ τ ] = [ M 1 L 2 T - 2 ] (C) We know, F = μ A u y where, F = force, μ = coefficient of viscosity A = area u y = rate of shear deformation ⇒ μ = F y A u ⇒ [ μ ] = [ M 1 L 1 T - 2 ] [ L ] [ L 2 ] [ L T - 1 ] ⇒ [ μ ] = [ M 1 L - 1 T - 1 ] (D) We know, F = G m 1 m 2 r 2 (Newton's law of gravitation) ⇒ G = F r 2 m 1 m 2 < ⇒ G = F r 2 m 1 m 2 ⇒ [ G ] = [ M 1 L 1 T - 2 ] [ L 2 ] [ M 2 ] ⇒ [ G ] = [ M - 1 L 3 T - 2 ] Hence, option D is correct.

q: The least count of a screw guage is 0.01 mm . If the pitch is increased by 75 % and number of divisions on the circular scale is reduced by 50 % , the new least count will be ________ × 10 - 3 mm
explanation: The least count (L.C.) of a screw gauge is given by: L.C. Pitch Number of Divisions on Circular Scale L.C. = Pitch Number of Divisions on Circular Scale Let the original pitch be p and the original number of divisions be n . Then, the original least count is: mm p n = 0.01 mm After modifications: The pitch is increased by 75%, so the new pitch is: new p new = p + 0.75 p = 1.75 p The number of divisions is reduced by 50%, so the new number of divisions is: new n new = 0.5 n The new least count is then: L.C. new new new L.C. new = p new n new = 1.75 p 0.5 n = 1.75 0.5 × p n = 3.5 × p n Substitute the original least count: L.C. new mm mm L.C. new = 3.5 × 0.01 mm = 0.035 mm Expressed in the form mm × 10 - 3 mm , this becomes: mm mm 0.035 mm = 35 × 10 - 3 mm Thus, the new least count is: mm 35 × 10 - 3 mm

q: A tiny metallic rectangular sheet has length and breadth of 5 mm and 2.5 mm , respectively. Using a specially designed screw gauge which has pitch of 0.75 mm and 15 divisions in the circular scale, you are asked to find the area of the sheet. In this measurement, the maximum fractional error will be x 100 where x is _______ .
explanation: $JEE Main 2025 (Online) 28th January Morning Shift Physics - Units & Measurements Question 43 English Explanation$ Given, L = 5 mm, B = 2.5 mm We know, least count of a screw gauge, Pitch length No. of division on circular scale L . C . = Pitch length No. of division on circular scale ⇒ L . C . = 0.75 15 = 0.05 m m We know, A = L B By taking l n on both sides, ⇒ ln ⁡ A = ln ⁡ L + ln ⁡ B by differentiating both sides, ⇒ d A A = d L L + d B B Here, d L = d B = 0.05 m m (L.C.) So fractional error, d A A = 0.05 5 + 0.05 2.5 = 1 100 + 2 100 = 3 100 = x 100 (given) Hence, x = 3 .

q: If B is magnetic field and μ 0 is permeability of free space, then the dimensions of ( B / μ 0 ) is
explanation: To determine the dimensions of ( B μ 0 ) , we start with the formula for the magnetic field B inside a solenoid: B = μ 0 n I where n = N L is the number of turns per unit length, I is the current, and μ 0 is the permeability of free space. Rearranging the formula gives: B μ 0 = n I = N L I From the above, the dimensions of ( B μ 0 ) are: [ B μ 0 ] = [ L - 1 A ] Thus, the dimensional formula of ( B μ 0 ) corresponds to A L - 1 A .

q: The pair of physical quantities not having the same dimensions is :

q: A physical quantity is related to four observables as follows : A physical quantity Q is related to four observables a , b , c , d as follows : Q = a b 4 c d where, a = ( 60 ± 3 ) Pa ; b = ( 20 ± 0.1 ) m ; c = ( 40 ± 0.2 ) Nsm - 2 and d = ( 50 ± 0.1 ) m , then the percentage error in Q is x 1000 , where x = _________ .
explanation: Given, Q = a b 4 c d a = ( 60 ± 3 ) P a ⇒ a = 60 P a , Δ a = 3 P a b = ( 20 ± 0.1 ) m ⇒ b = 20 m , Δ b = 0.1 m c = ( 40 ± 0.2 ) N s m - 2 ⇒ c = 40 N s m - 2 , Δ c = 0.2 N s m - 2 d = ( 50 ± 0.1 ) m ⇒ d = 50 m , Δ d = 0.1 m As, Q = a b 4 c d by taking ln on both sides, ln ⁡ Q = ln ⁡ a + u ln ⁡ b - ln ⁡ c - ln ⁡ d Now, by differentiating, d Q Q = d a a + 4 d b b - d c c - d d d So, maximum fractional error in Q is given by, Δ Q Q = Δ a a + 4 Δ b b + Δ c c + Δ d d ⇒ Δ Q Q = 3 60 + 4 ( 0.1 20 ) + 0.2 40 + 0.1 40 = 1 20 + 1 50 + 1 200 + 1 500 ⇒ Δ Q Q = 50 + 20 + 5 + 2 1000 = 77 1000 Hence the % error in Q = Δ Q Q × 100 % = 7700 1000 % = x 1000 (given) So, x = 7700

q: Match List - I with List - II . List - I List - II (A) Mass density (I) [ML $^{2}$ T $^{-3}$ ] (B) Impulse (II) [MLT $^{-1}$ ] (C) Power (III) [ML $^{2}$ T $^{0}$ ] (D) Moment of inertia (IV) [ML $^{-3}$ T $^{0}$ ] Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation: (A) Mass density: Mass density is defined as mass per unit volume. The dimension of mass is [ M ] . The dimension of volume is [ L 3 ] . Therefore, the dimension of mass density is [ M ] [ L 3 ] = [ M L - 3 T 0 ] . Matches with (IV). (B) Impulse: Impulse is defined as the change in momentum, or force multiplied by time. Impulse = Force x Time The dimension of force is [ M L T - 2 ] . The dimension of time is [ T ] . Therefore, the dimension of impulse is [ M L T - 2 ] ⋅ [ T ] = [ M L T - 1 ] . Matches with (II). (C) Power: Power is defined as the rate of doing work or energy per unit time. Power = Work / Time The dimension of work (or energy) is [ M L 2 T - 2 ] . The dimension of time is [ T ] . Therefore, the dimension of power is [ M L 2 T - 2 ] [ T ] = [ M L 2 T - 3 ] . Matches with (I). (D) Moment of inertia: Moment of inertia is defined as I = m r 2 , where m is mass and r is the distance from the axis of rotation. The dimension of mass is [ M ] . The dimension of distance squared is [ L 2 ] . Therefore, the dimension of moment of inertia is [ M L 2 T 0 ] . Matches with (III). So, the correct matches are: (A) - (IV) (B) - (II) (C) - (I) (D) - (III) Therefore, the correct option is D .

q: A quantity Q is formulated as X - 2 Y + 3 2 Z - 2 5 . X, Y, and Z are independent parameters which have fractional errors of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5, respectively in measurement. The maximum fractional error of Q is
explanation: The quantity Q is expressed as Q = X - 2 Y + 3 2 Z - 2 5 . The variables X , Y , and Z are independent parameters with fractional errors of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5, respectively. To determine the maximum fractional error in Q , we can use the following method: Fractional error in Fractional error in Q = | - 2 | Δ X X + | 3 2 | Δ Y Y + | - 2 5 | Δ Z Z Substituting the fractional errors into the equation: = 2 × 0.1 + 3 2 × 0.2 + 2 5 × 0.5 Calculating each term gives: = 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.2 Summing these values results in: = 0.7 Thus, the maximum fractional error in Q is 0.7.

q: The position of a particle moving on x -axis is given by x ( t ) = A sin ⁡ t + B cos 2 ⁡ t + C t 2 + D , where t is time. The dimension of A B C D is
explanation: Let's analyze the position function step by step. The particle's position is given by: x ( t ) = A sin ⁡ t + B cos 2 ⁡ t + C t 2 + D , where t represents time, which has the dimension T . Since the overall dimensions of position x ( t ) must be length ( L ) , each term in the expression must also have dimensions of length. Term A sin ⁡ t : The sine function, sin ⁡ t , is dimensionless (its argument must be dimensionless, and by convention, if t is in an appropriate unit where the argument is dimensionless, the function itself is dimensionless). Therefore, A must carry the dimension of length: [ A ] = L . Term B cos 2 ⁡ t : Similarly, the cosine function is dimensionless, and so is its square. Thus, B must have the dimension of length: [ B ] = L . Term C t 2 : Here, t 2 has the dimension T 2 . To have the overall term with the dimension L , we require: [ C ] × [ T 2 ] = L . So, the dimension of C must be: [ C ] = L T - 2 . Term D : This is a constant term added to position, so it must also have the dimension of length: [ D ] = L . Now, we are asked to find the dimension of: A B C D . The dimensions of A , B , and C are: [ A ] = L , [ B ] = L , [ C ] = L T - 2 . Multiplying them together: [ A B C ] = L × L × ( L T - 2 ) = L 3 T - 2 . Since [ D ] = L , dividing by D gives: [ A B C ] [ D ] = L 3 T - 2 L = L 2 T - 2 . Thus, the dimension of A B C D is: L 2 T - 2 . The correct answer is Option C.

q: Given a charge q , current I and permeability of vacuum μ o ∗ . Which of the following quantity has the dimension of momentum ?
explanation: Let's look at the units (dimensions) of the given quantities: Charge Q has units of ampere × time ( A ⋅ T ) Current I has units of ampere ( A ) Permeability of vacuum μ 0 has units of mass × length × time - 2 × ampere - 2 ( M L T - 2 A - 2 ) Momentum has units of mass × length × time - 1 ( M L T - 1 ) We want to combine Q , μ 0 , and I so that the result has the same units as momentum ( M L T - 1 ) . Suppose the answer is Q x μ 0 y I z . Let's write out the units for this: Q x μ 0 y I z = ( A T ) x ( M L T - 2 A - 2 ) y ( A ) z Combine the powers of each unit: Mass (M):      exponent = y Length (L):    exponent = y Time (T):      exponent = x - 2 y Ampere (A):    exponent = x - 2 y + z We want these to match the units of momentum, M 1 L 1 T - 1 : So we set up: y = 1 (so mass and length exponents match momentum) x - 2 y = - 1 (time exponent) x - 2 y + z = 0 (ampere exponent cancels out) Solve these equations: First, y = 1 . Second, x - 2 ( 1 ) = - 1 ⟹ x = 1 . Third, x - 2 ( 1 ) + z = 0 ⟹ 1 - 2 + z = 0 ⟹ z = 1 . The required combination is x = 1 , y = 1 , z = 1 : So, the quantity with the dimension of momentum is Q μ 0 I .

q: For an experimental expression y = 32.3 × 1125 27.4 , where all the digits are significant. Then to report the value of y we should write
explanation: sig figs sig figs sig figs 32.3 ( 3 sig figs ) , 1125 ( 4 sig figs ) , 27.4 ( 3 sig figs ) In multiplication and division, the final answer must be reported with the same number of significant figures as the factor with the fewest significant figures, which here is 3. First, calculate the unrounded result: y = 32.3 × 1125 27.4 ≈ 36337.5 27.4 ≈ 1326.186 Expressing 1326.186 in scientific notation: 1326.186 ≈ 1.326186 × 10 3 Rounding to 3 significant figures: The first three significant digits are 1, 3, and 2. Considering the fourth digit (6) causes the last significant digit to round up. Thus, 1.326186 × 10 3 ≈ 1.33 × 10 3 In standard decimal form, this is written as: 1330 Hence, the final reported value is: y = 1330

q: The maximum percentage error in the measurment of density of a wire is [Given, mass of wire = ( 0.60 ± 0.003 ) g radius of wire = ( 0.50 ± 0.01 ) cm length of wire = ( 10.00 ± 0.05 ) cm ]
explanation: To determine the maximum percentage error in the density of the wire, follow these steps: The density of the wire is given by the formula for a cylinder: ρ = m π r 2 l When calculating the maximum percentage (relative) error, you add the relative errors from each variable, taking into account the power to which each variable is raised. Thus, for density: Δ ρ ρ = Δ m m + 2 Δ r r + Δ l l Here: Δ m m is the relative error in the mass. 2 Δ r r is due to the radius being squared. Δ l l is the relative error in the length. Now plug in the given values: Mass: g m = 0.60 g with an error g Δ m = 0.003 g Δ m m = 0.003 0.60 = 0.005 or 0.5%. Radius: cm r = 0.50 cm with an error cm Δ r = 0.01 cm Δ r r = 0.01 0.50 = 0.02 or 2%. Since the radius is squared in the formula, multiply by 2: 2 Δ r r = 2 × 0.02 = 0.04 or 4%. Length: cm l = 10.00 cm with an error cm Δ l = 0.05 cm Δ l l = 0.05 10.00 = 0.005 or 0.5%. Sum these contributions to find the overall maximum relative error: Δ ρ ρ = 0.005 + 0.04 + 0.005 = 0.05 Converting this relative error into a percentage gives: 0.05 × 100 % = 5 % Thus, the maximum percentage error in the density of the wire is 5%, which corresponds to Option C.

q: The dimension of μ 0 ϵ 0 is equal to that of: ( μ 0 = Vacuum permeability and ϵ 0 = Vacuum permittivity)
explanation: The dimension of μ 0 ϵ 0 can be understood as follows: Vacuum permeability ( μ 0 ) and vacuum permittivity ( ϵ 0 ) relate to the properties of inductance and capacitance, respectively, in a vacuum. We start by considering the formulas for inductance ( L ) and capacitance ( C ): Inductance ( L ) is given by: Number of turns Area Length L = μ 0 ⋅ Number of turns ⋅ Area Length Capacitance ( C ) is given by: Area Distance C = Area ⋅ ϵ 0 Distance From these, the ratio L C can be expressed as μ 0 ϵ 0 : L C ∝ μ 0 ϵ 0 Taking the square root of this ratio, we have: μ 0 ϵ 0 ∝ L C This simplifies further to considering the relationship between time constant ( τ ) and resistance ( R ): L C = τ R ( τ / R ) = R 2 Thus, by taking the square root: μ 0 ϵ 0 = R In conclusion, the dimension of μ 0 ϵ 0 is equivalent to the dimension of resistance, R .

q: The energy of a system is given as E ( t ) = α 3 e - β t , where t is the time and β = 0.3 s - 1 . The errors in the measurement of α and t are 1.2 % and 1.6 % , respectively. At t = 5 s , maximum percentage error in the energy is :
explanation: We start with the energy function: E ( t ) = α 3 e - β t , where s β = 0.3 s - 1 . Step 1. Take the Logarithm Taking the logarithm of both sides: ln ⁡ E = 3 ln ⁡ α - β t . Step 2. Differentiate to Find the Relative Error Differentiate both sides: d E E = 3 d α α - β d t . For maximum error estimation, we consider the absolute values and sum the contributions: | Δ E E | ≈ 3 | Δ α α | + β | Δ t | . Step 3. Plug in the Given Errors The percentage error in α is 1.2 % (i.e., Δ α / α = 0.012 ). The percentage error in t is 1.6 % , so for s t = 5 s : s Δ t = 0.016 × 5 = 0.08 s . Now substitute these values: | Δ E E | ≈ 3 ( 0.012 ) + 0.3 ( 0.08 ) . Calculating each term: 3 ( 0.012 ) = 0.036 (or 3.6 % ), 0.3 ( 0.08 ) = 0.024 (or 2.4 % ). Step 4. Compute the Total Maximum Percentage Error Add the contributions: | Δ E E | ≈ 0.036 + 0.024 = 0.06 , which is 6 % . Thus, the maximum percentage error in the energy at s t = 5 s is: 6 % Answer: Option A (6%).

q: If μ 0 and ϵ 0 are the permeability and permittivity of free space, respectively, then the dimension of ( 1 μ 0 ϵ 0 ) is :
explanation: The expression 1 μ 0 ϵ 0 is related to the speed of light c , given by the equation: c = 1 μ 0 ϵ 0 Therefore, we have: 1 μ 0 ϵ 0 = c 2 The speed of light c has the dimensions of L T L T - 1 , where L L is the dimension of length and T T is the dimension of time. Thus, when squared, the dimensions become: L T L T c 2 = ( L T - 1 ) 2 = L 2 T - 2 Hence, the dimensions of 1 μ 0 ϵ 0 are L T L 2 T - 2 .

q: In an electromagnetic system, the quantity representing the ratio of electric flux and magnetic flux has dimension of M P L Q T R A S , where value of ' Q ' and ' R ' are
explanation: To solve this problem, we need to find the dimensions of the electric flux and the magnetic flux, and then take their ratio. Determine the dimension of electric flux (Φₑ): Electric flux is defined as Φ E = ∫ S E ⋅ d A . The electric field E has dimensions: Force Charge [ E ] = Force Charge . Since force has dimensions M L T - 2 , and charge (in SI) has dimensions A T , we get: [ E ] = M L T - 2 A T = M L T - 3 A - 1 . The area element d A has dimensions L 2 . Thus, the electric flux has dimensions: [ Φ E ] = [ E ] [ A ] = M L T - 3 A - 1 × L 2 = M L 3 T - 3 A - 1 . Determine the dimension of magnetic flux (Φ_B): Magnetic flux is defined as Φ B = ∫ S B ⋅ d A . The magnetic field B has dimensions determined via the Lorentz force law (using F = q v B ), giving: [ B ] = F q v = M L T - 2 ( A T ) ( L T - 1 ) = M T - 2 A - 1 . Again, the area element has dimensions L 2 . So the magnetic flux has dimensions: [ Φ B ] = [ B ] [ A ] = ( M T - 2 A - 1 ) ( L 2 ) = M L 2 T - 2 A - 1 . Find the ratio of electric flux to magnetic flux: Taking the ratio: Φ E Φ B = M L 3 T - 3 A - 1 M L 2 T - 2 A - 1 . Canceling like terms: Mass ( M ) cancels. Current ( A - 1 ) cancels. For length, L 3 - 2 = L 1 . For time, T - 3 - ( - 2 ) = T - 1 . Therefore, the ratio has dimensions: L 1 T - 1 . This means in the expression M P L Q T R A S , the exponents for length and time are Q = 1 and R = - 1. Looking at the options provided, this corresponds to Option D: ( 1 , - 1 ) . Thus, the answer is Option D.

q: A physical quantity C is related to four other quantities p , q , r and s as follows C = p q 2 r 3 s The percentage errors in the measurement of p , q , r and s are 1 % , 2 % , 3 % and 2 % , respectively. The percentage error in the measurement of C will be__________%
explanation: To determine the percentage error in the measurement of C , which is related to p , q , r , and s as: C = p q 2 r 3 s we first express it in terms of powers: C = p 1 q 2 r - 3 s - 1 / 2 The percentage error in C can be calculated using the formula for the propagation of error, which is: ( Δ C C ) max = | Δ p p | + 2 | Δ q q | + 3 | Δ r r | + 1 2 | Δ s s | Given the percentage errors for p , q , r , and s are 1 % , 2 % , 3 % , and 2 % respectively, we substitute these values into the formula: ( Δ C C ) max = 1 % + 2 × 2 % + 3 × 3 % + 1 2 × 2 % Calculating each part: Contribution from p : 1 % Contribution from q : 4 % (since 2 × 2 % = 4 % ) Contribution from r : 9 % (since 3 × 3 % = 9 % ) Contribution from s : 1 % (since 1 2 × 2 % = 1 % ) Adding these contributions together: 1 % + 4 % + 9 % + 1 % = 15 % Thus, the maximum percentage error in the measurement of C is 15 % .

q: In finding out refractive index of glass slab the following observations were made through travelling microscope 50 vernier scale division = 49 MSD ; 20 divisions on main scale in each cm For mark on paper MSR MSR = 8.45 cm , VC = 26 For mark on paper seen through slab MSR = 7.12 cm , VC = 41 For powder particle on the top surface of the glass slab MSR MSR = 4.05 cm , VC = 1 (MSR = Main Scale Reading, VC = Vernier Coincidence) Refractive index of the glass slab is :
explanation: To find the refractive index of the glass slab, we first need to calculate the actual readings using the Main Scale Reading (MSR) and Vernier Coincidence (VC), and understand how to translate these values into a measurement of the refractive index. We will start by calculating the Least Count (LC) of the vernier calipers. The least count (LC) of the traveling microscope is given by the formula: LC MSD VSD LC = MSD - VSD Where MSD is the value of one main scale division and VSD is the value of one vernier scale division in terms of the main scale. We are told 50 vernier scale divisions equal 49 main scale divisions (MSD), so 1 VSD is 49 50 of an MSD. Given that 20 divisions on the main scale represent 1 cm, each main scale division (MSD) represents: MSD cm cm 1 MSD = 1 cm 20 = 0.05 cm Therefore, the least count (LC) of the microscope is: LC cm cm cm cm cm LC = 0.05 cm - ( 49 50 × 0.05 cm ) = 0.05 cm - 0.049 cm = 0.001 cm Now, using the least count to find the total reading (TR) from both supplied observations: For the mark on paper, the total reading (TR) is: TR MSR VC LC TR = MSR + ( VC × LC ) TR paper cm cm cm cm cm TR paper = 8.45 cm + ( 26 × 0.001 cm ) = 8.45 cm + 0.026 cm = 8.476 cm For the mark on the paper seen through the slab, the total reading is: TR seen through slab cm cm cm cm cm TR seen through slab = 7.12 cm + ( 41 × 0.001 cm ) = 7.12 cm + 0.041 cm = 7.161 cm For the powder particle on the top surface of the glass slab: TR top surface cm cm cm cm cm TR top surface = 4.05 cm + ( 1 × 0.001 cm ) = 4.05 cm + 0.001 cm = 4.051 cm The real depth (RD) observed directly is the difference between the first and third observations (mark on paper and powder particle on top surface): RD TR paper TR top surface cm cm cm RD = TR paper - TR top surface = 8.476 cm - 4.051 cm = 4.425 cm The apparent depth (AD) when viewed through the slab is the difference between the second and third observations: AD TR seen through slab TR top surface cm cm cm AD = TR seen through slab - TR top surface = 7.161 cm - 4.051 cm = 3.11 cm The refractive index ( n ) of the glass slab can be found using the formula: Real Depth (RD) Apparent Depth (AD) cm cm n = Real Depth (RD) Apparent Depth (AD) = 4.425 cm 3.11 cm Calculating this gives: n = 4.425 3.11 ≈ 1.42 Therefore, the refractive index of the glass slab is approximately 1.42, which corresponds to Option D.

q: To find the spring constant ( k ) of a spring experimentally, a student commits 2 % positive error in the measurement of time and 1 % negative error in measurement of mass. The percentage error in determining value of k is :
explanation: To determine the spring constant k of a spring experimentally, we can use the formula derived from Hooke's Law and the period of oscillation for a mass-spring system: T = 2 π m k Here, T is the period of oscillation, m is the mass, and k is the spring constant. Rearranging the formula to solve for k , we get: k = 4 π 2 m T 2 To find the error in k , we have to consider the errors in both the measurements of T and m . Let's denote the percentage errors as follows: Δ T / T ⋅ 100 % = 2 % (positive error) Δ m / m ⋅ 100 % = - 1 % (negative error) According to the rules of error propagation, the relative error in k can be found by adding the relative errors in the measurements, each multiplied by the respective powers to which they affect k . Since T is squared in the denominator and m is linear in the numerator, the calculation is as follows: Δ k k = | - 2 ⋅ Δ T T | + | 1 ⋅ Δ m m | Substituting the percentage errors: Δ k k = | - 2 ⋅ 0.02 | + | 1 ⋅ ( - 0.01 ) | Δ k k = 0.04 + 0.01 Δ k k = 0.05 Thus, the percentage error in determining the value of k is: Δ k k ⋅ 100 % = 5 % The correct answer is Option A: 5%

q: If ϵ o is the permittivity of free space and E is the electric field, then ϵ o E 2 has the dimensions :
explanation: To determine the dimensions of ϵ o E 2 , we need to first understand the dimensional formulas of each component in the expression. 1. Permittivity of free space, ϵ o : The permittivity of free space has the dimensions: [ ϵ o ] = [ M - 1 L - 3 T 4 A 2 ] . 2. Electric field, E : The electric field E has the dimensions: [ E ] = [ M L T - 3 A - 1 ] . Now, let's calculate the dimensions of ϵ o E 2 : [ ϵ o E 2 ] = [ ϵ o ] [ E ] 2 = [ M - 1 L - 3 T 4 A 2 ] ( [ M L T - 3 A - 1 ] 2 ] = [ M - 1 L - 3 T 4 A 2 ] ( [ M 2 L 2 T - 6 A - 2 ] ] Combining the dimensions: [ ϵ o E 2 ] = [ M - 1 L - 3 T 4 A 2 ] [ M 2 L 2 T - 6 A - 2 ] = [ M - 1 + 2 L - 3 + 2 T 4 - 6 A 2 - 2 ] = [ M L - 1 T - 2 ] Therefore, the dimensions of ϵ o E 2 are [ M L - 1 T - 2 ] , which corresponds to Option A. Hence, the correct answer is Option A: [ M L - 1 T - 2 ] .

q: While measuring diameter of wire using screw gauge the following readings were noted. Main scale reading is 1 mm and circular scale reading is equal to 42 divisions. Pitch of screw gauge is 1 mm and it has 100 divisions on circular scale. The diameter of the wire is x 50 mm . The value of x is :
explanation: To determine the diameter of the wire using a screw gauge, we employ the formula: Total reading MSR CSR LC Total reading = MSR + ( CSR × LC ) where: MSR (Main Scale Reading) is the value read directly from the main scale in mm. CSR (Circular Scale Reading) is the number of divisions observed on the circular scale. LC (Least Count) is the value of one division on the circular scale, calculated as Pitch Number of divisions on the circular scale Pitch Number of divisions on the circular scale . Given: Main Scale Reading (MSR) = 1 mm Circular Scale Reading (CSR) = 42 divisions Pitch of screw gauge = 1 mm Number of divisions on circular scale = 100 First, we find the Least Count (LC): Pitch Number of divisions on the circular scale L C = Pitch Number of divisions on the circular scale = 1 mm 100 = 0.01 mm Then we calculate the total measurement of the diameter of the wire: Total reading MSR CSR LC Total reading = MSR + ( CSR × LC ) = 1 mm + ( 42 × 0.01 mm ) = 1 mm + 0.42 mm = 1.42 mm Given that the diameter of the wire is also represented as x 50 mm , we can equate this to our found total reading: 1.42 mm = x 50 mm Solving for x : 1.42 = x 50 x = 1.42 × 50 x = 71 Therefore, the value of x is 71, which corresponds to Option B: 71.

q: Match List - I with List - II . List I (Number) List II (Significant figure) (A) 1001 (I) 3 (B) 010.1 (II) 4 (C) 100.100 (III) 5 (D) 0.0010010 (IV) 6 Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation: Significant figures in a number represent the digits that carry meaning contributing to its precision. This includes all digits except: All leading zeros. Trailing zeros only when they are merely placeholders to indicate the scale of the number (they must not be significant if there is no decimal point). Here is the explanation for the significant figures of each number in List I: (A) 1001 - All the digits in this number are significant because there are no leading or trailing zeros acting as placeholders. Hence, this number has 4 significant figures . (B) 010.1 - The leading zero is not significant, but the zero between 1 and the decimal point, the 1 itself, and the 1 after the decimal point are all significant. So, this number has 3 significant figures . (C) 100.100 - Here, the number has zeros which are significant since they are between significant digits or after the decimal point. This makes all the zeros and the 1s significant. Therefore, the number has 6 significant figures . (D) 0.0010010 - The leading zeros are not significant, but the three zeros within and at the end of the number are significant because they are between significant digits or at the end of the number after the decimal. Therefore, this number has 5 significant figures . Given List I and List II, the correct matching based on the explanation of significant figures is as follows: Option A (A) - II (1001 has 4 significant figures) (B) - I (010.1 has 3 significant figures) (C) - IV (100.100 has 6 significant figures) (D) - III (0.0010010 has 5 significant figures) Thus, the correct answer is Option A.

q: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with a particle of mass m and energy E is h / 2 m E . The dimensional formula for Planck's constant is :
explanation: To determine the dimensional formula for Planck's constant, we will start by analyzing the given de-Broglie wavelength equation: λ = h 2 m E Here, λ is the wavelength, h is the Planck's constant, m is the mass of the particle, and E is the energy of the particle. First, let's derive the dimensional formula for each term involved: 1. Wavelength λ has the dimensional formula of length [ L ] . 2. Mass m has the dimensional formula M [ M ] . 3. Energy E has the dimensional formula of work, which is force times distance: MLT ML T [ E ] = [ F ] [ L ] = [ MLT - 2 ] [ L ] = [ ML 2 T - 2 ] . Now, let's rewrite the equation in terms of the dimensions: M ML T [ L ] = [ h ] 2 [ M ] [ ML 2 T - 2 ] Simplifying inside the square root: M M L T [ L ] = [ h ] 2 [ M ] [ M ] [ L 2 T - 2 ] M L T [ L ] = [ h ] 2 [ M 2 ] [ L 2 T - 2 ] Since the constants like 2 do not affect the dimensional formula, we can simplify further: M L T [ L ] = [ h ] [ M L T - 1 ] Cross multiplying to solve for the dimensional formula of h : M L T [ h ] = [ L ] [ M L T - 1 ] M L T [ h ] = [ M L 2 T - 1 ] Therefore, the dimensional formula for Planck's constant h is: ML T [ ML 2 T - 1 ] Hence, the correct option is: Option C [ ML 2 T - 1 ]

q: A physical quantity Q is found to depend on quantities a , b , c by the relation Q = a 4 b 3 c 2 . The percentage error in a , b and c are 3 % , 4 % and 5 % respectively. Then, the percentage error in Q is :
The dimensional formula of angular impulse is :
explanation: Angular impulse is given when a torque is applied for a certain amount of time. The angular impulse changes the angular momentum of an object and has the same dimensions as angular momentum. The dimensional formula for torque τ is the same as that for work, since torque is a kind of rotational work, which is given by force times distance (or in rotational terms, it can be considered as force times lever arm). The dimensional formula for force is [ M L T - 2 ] , and when multiplied by distance [ L ] , we get: [ Torque ] = [ M L T - 2 ] × [ L ] = [ M L 2 T - 2 ] Now, angular impulse is torque times time, so we multiply the dimension of torque by time [ T ] : [ Angular Impulse ] = [ M L 2 T - 2 ] × [ T ] = [ M L 2 T - 1 ] So the correct dimensional formula for angular impulse is given by Option C, which is [ M L 2 T - 1 ] .

q:
Applying the principle of homogeneity of dimensions, determine which one is correct,
where T is time period, G is gravitational constant, M is mass, r is radius of orbit.
options: A. T 2 = 4 π 2 r G M 2
C. T 2 = 4 π 2 r 3 G M
D. T 2 = 4 π 2 r 2 G M
explanation: To determine which option is correct based on the principle of homogeneity of dimensions, we need to ensure that both sides of the equation have the same dimensions. The time period ( T ) is measured in units of time ( T ), the gravitational constant ( G ) has units of m kg s m 3 kg - 1 s - 2 , mass ( M ) has units of mass ( M ), and the radius of orbit ( r ) has units of length ( L ). Let's analyze each option: Option A: T 2 = 4 π 2 r G M 2 The dimensions of the right-hand side of the equation are L ( L 3 M T 2 ) M 2 = L L 3 M - 1 T - 2 M 2 = L L 3 T - 2 = L - 2 T 2 , which do not match with T 2 (time squared). Thus, Option A is incorrect. Option B: T 2 = 4 π 2 r 3 The dimensions of the right-hand side are L 3 , which clearly do not match the dimensions T 2 of the squared time period. So, Option B is incorrect. Option C: T 2 = 4 π 2 r 3 G M The dimensions of the right-hand side of the equation are L 3 ( L 3 M T 2 ) M = L 3 L 3 T - 2 = T 2 , which match the dimensions of the squared time period T 2 . Therefore, Option C is correct based on the principle of homogeneity of dimensions. Option D: T 2 = 4 π 2 r 2 G M The dimensions of the right-hand side are L 2 ( L 3 M T 2 ) M = L 2 L 3 M - 1 T - 2 M = L - 1 T 2 , which do not match with T 2 (time squared). Hence, Option D is incorrect. Thus, based on the principle of homogeneity of dimensions, Option C is the correct one: T 2 = 4 π 2 r 3 G M . This equation also corresponds to Kepler's third law of planetary motion, which relates the orbital period of a planet to its orbital radius, considering the mass of the central body.

q: There are 100 divisions on the circular scale of a screw gauge of pitch 1 mm . With no measuring quantity in between the jaws, the zero of the circular scale lies 5 divisions below the reference line. The diameter of a wire is then measured using this screw gauge. It is found that 4 linear scale divisions are clearly visible while 60 divisions on circular scale coincide with the reference line. The diameter of the wire is :
explanation: Pitch of the screw gauge : 1 mm Number of divisions on the circular scale : 100 divisions Zero error : The zero of the circular scale lies 5 divisions below the reference line, indicating a positive zero error. Measurement data : 4 linear scale divisions are visible. 60 divisions on the circular scale coincide with the reference line. Step-by-Step Calculation Least Count of the screw gauge : Least Count Pitch Number of Divisions on Circular Scale mm mm Least Count = Pitch Number of Divisions on Circular Scale = 1 mm 100 = 0.01 mm Main Scale Reading (MSR) : The linear scale shows 4 divisions, so the main scale reading is: MSR mm MSR = 4 mm Circular Scale Reading (CSR) : 60 divisions coincide with the reference line, so the circular scale reading is: CSR Least Count mm mm CSR = 60 × Least Count = 60 × 0.01 mm = 0.60 mm Zero Error : The zero error is 5 divisions below the reference line, indicating a positive zero error: Zero Error Least Count mm mm Zero Error = + 5 × Least Count = + 5 × 0.01 mm = + 0.05 mm Total Reading without considering zero error : Total Reading (without zero error) MSR CSR mm mm mm Total Reading (without zero error) = MSR + CSR = 4 mm + 0.60 mm = 4.60 mm Corrected Reading considering zero error : Since the zero error is positive, we subtract it from the total reading: Corrected Reading Total Reading (without zero error) Zero Error mm mm mm Corrected Reading = Total Reading (without zero error) - Zero Error = 4.60 mm - 0.05 mm = 4.55 mm Conclusion The diameter of the wire is: Option C: 4.55 mm

q: A vernier callipers has 20 divisions on the vernier scale, which coincides with th 19 th division on the main scale. The least count of the instrument is 0.1 mm . One main scale division is equal to ________ mm.
explanation: To determine the value of one main scale division (MSD) for the given vernier calipers, we'll analyze the relationship between the main scale divisions, vernier scale divisions, and the least count of the instrument. Given: Number of vernier scale divisions (n): 20 Vernier scale coincides with the 19th division on the main scale. Least count (LC): mm 0.1 mm Understanding the Vernier Calipers Configuration: Relation between Vernier and Main Scale Divisions: The total length of the vernier scale (comprising 20 divisions) coincides with the length of 19 main scale divisions. Mathematically, this is expressed as: VSD MSD n × VSD = ( n - 1 ) × MSD where: VSD VSD is the value of one vernier scale division. MSD MSD is the value of one main scale division. Calculating the Value of One Vernier Scale Division ( VSD VSD ): VSD MSD VSD = ( n - 1 ) × MSD n Least Count (LC): The least count is the smallest measurement that can be accurately read using the instrument. It is calculated as: LC MSD VSD LC = MSD - VSD Substituting VSD VSD from step 2: LC MSD MSD LC = MSD - ( ( n - 1 ) × MSD n ) LC MSD LC = MSD ( 1 - n - 1 n ) LC MSD LC = MSD ( n - ( n - 1 ) n ) LC MSD LC = MSD ( 1 n ) Solving for One Main Scale Division ( MSD MSD ): Rearranging the equation: LC MSD LC = MSD n MSD LC MSD = LC × n Substituting the given values ( LC mm LC = 0.1 mm , n = 20 ): MSD mm MSD = 0.1 mm × 20 MSD mm MSD = 2 mm Conclusion: The value of one main scale division is 2 mm , which corresponds to Option B .
